back_translation: As for me, in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to strengthen and protect him.

Now I will tell you the truth. Behold, three more kings will arise in Persia, and a fourth will be far richer than all of them. When he grows strong through his wealth, he will stir up everyone against the kingdom of Greece.
Then a mighty king will arise, who will rule with great authority and do as he pleases.

But as soon as he is established, his kingdom will be broken and divided toward the four winds of heaven. It will not go to his descendants, nor have the authority he had, for his kingdom will be uprooted and given to others besides them.
The king of the South will grow strong, but one of his commanders will grow stronger and rule his own kingdom with great authority.

After some years they will form an alliance, and the daughter of the king of the South will come to the king of the North to make a peace agreement. But she will not retain her power, nor will his line endure; she will be given up, along with those who brought her, her father, and the one who supported her in those times.
But a shoot from her roots will arise in his place. He will come against the army and enter the fortress of the king of the North, fighting and prevailing.
He will even carry off their gods, with their metal images and precious vessels of silver and gold, to Egypt; and for some years he will refrain from attacking the king of the North.
The king of the North will invade the kingdom of the king of the South and then return to his own land.
But his sons will wage war and gather a great multitude of forces, which will advance, sweeping through like a flood, and will again carry the battle as far as the fortress of the king of the South.
The king of the South will be enraged and march out to fight the king of the North, who will raise a great army, but that army will be given into the hand of his enemy.
When the army is carried off, the heart of the king of the South will be proud, and he will kill tens of thousands, but he will not have a lasting victory.
The king of the North will return and raise an army larger than the first, and after some years he will advance with a great army and abundant supplies.
In those times many will rise up against the king of the South, and violent men among your own people will rise up to fulfill the vision, but they will fail.
The king of the North will come, build a siege ramp, and capture a fortified city. The forces of the South will not stand; even their best troops will have no strength to resist.
The invader will do as he pleases, and no one will stand against him. He will establish himself in the Beautiful Land, with destruction in his hand.

He will resolve to come with the strength of his whole kingdom and make an agreement with the king of the South. He will give him a daughter in marriage to overthrow the kingdom, but the plan will not succeed or help him.
Then he will turn to the coastlands and capture many of them. But a commander will end his arrogance and turn that shame back upon him.
Then he will turn back toward the fortresses of his own land, but he will stumble and fall and be no more.
One will arise in his place who sends out a tax collector for the glory of the kingdom, but within a few days he will be destroyed, not in anger or in battle.
A despicable person will arise in his place, to whom royal honor was not given. He will come in a time of peace and seize the kingdom by intrigue.
A flood of forces will be swept away before him and destroyed, along with a prince of the covenant.
After making an alliance with him, he will act deceitfully; he will rise to power with only a few people.

In a time of peace he will invade the richest provinces and do what his fathers and forefathers never did. He will distribute plunder, spoil, and wealth among his followers, and he will plot against the strongholds—but only for a time.

He will rouse his power and courage against the king of the South with a great army. The king of the South will mobilize a very large and powerful army for war, but he will not stand, because plots will be devised against him.
Those who eat from his provisions will destroy him; his army will be swept away, and many will fall slain.
The two kings, their hearts bent on evil, will sit at one table and speak lies to each other, but it will not succeed, for the end will still come at the appointed time.
The king of the North will return to his land with great wealth, but his heart will be set against the holy covenant. He will act and then return to his own land.
At the appointed time he will return and invade the South again, but this time it will not be like the former.

For ships from Kittim will come against him, and he will lose heart and turn back. Then he will rage against the holy covenant and act, and he will turn back and favor those who forsake the holy covenant.
His forces will rise up and defile the temple fortress, abolish the daily sacrifice, and set up the abomination that causes desolation.
With flattery he will corrupt those who violate the covenant, but the people who know their God will stand firm and resist him.
The wise among the people will instruct many, though for a time they will fall by sword and flame, by captivity and plunder.
When they fall, they will receive a little help, and many will join them insincerely.
Some of the wise will fall, so that they may be refined, purified, and made white until the time of the end, for the appointed time is still to come.

The king will do as he pleases. He will exalt and magnify himself above every god and speak astonishing things against the God of gods. He will prosper until the wrath is completed, for what is decreed must be accomplished.
He will show no regard for the gods of his fathers, nor for the one desired by women, nor for any god, for he will magnify himself above them all.
Instead he will honor a god of fortresses—a god his fathers did not know—with gold, silver, precious stones, and costly gifts.
He will attack the strongest fortresses with the help of a foreign god, and he will greatly honor those who acknowledge him, making them rulers over many and distributing land as a reward.

At the time of the end, the king of the South will clash with him, and the king of the North will storm against him with chariots, horsemen, and many ships. He will invade many countries and sweep through like a flood.
He will also invade the Beautiful Land, and many countries will fall. But these will escape from his hand: Edom, Moab, and the leaders of the Ammonites.
He will extend his power over many countries, and even the land of Egypt will not escape.
He will control the treasures of gold and silver and all the riches of Egypt, and the Libyans and Cushites will submit to him.
But news from the east and the north will alarm him, and he will go out with great fury to destroy and annihilate many.
He will pitch his royal tents between the sea and the beautiful holy mountain, but he will come to his end, and no one will help him.
